column2: Enter project number, team, title, repo, Type (A/B), Graph? (Y/N), and Edge/Mobile? (Y/N).
Enter 0, 1, or 2 for each applicable criterion. Leave A-only, B-only, or Graph-only cells blank when they do not apply.
Each rubric header cell in the Gradebook includes a note/comment with the exact 0 / 1 / 2 anchor. The Rubric_Guide tab contains the same anchors in table form.
Type req flag checks essential mandatory requirements from the announcement. Graph req flag checks graph-specific hard requirements. Review shows the quickest issue to look at.
The announcement requires at least 3 of 4 topics to be addressed. The workbook counts these in the 'RML topics met' column.
Each bonus criterion can add up to +1 point. Total bonus = (BX1 + BX2 + BX3) / 2, for a max of +3.
Base grade is weighted to 100 using the category weights from last semester. Final % = Base grade + Bonus. The base grade stays blank until all applicable base-rubric cells are filled.

Option A: a Streamlit app that simply wraps a pretrained model without baseline or analysis is insufficient. Option B: if the outcome is not plausibly publishable, the project is rejected, not downgraded. Graph projects must use the graph as a core modeling object and compare to a non-graph baseline.

column1: 9. What this workbook intentionally does not automate
column2: Peer-relative or competitive cohort ranking is not automated here, so the base rubric stays as objective and defensible as possible.
